coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots modification of yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres for boosted lithium storage performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Minghong
Chen, Hengqiao
Lv, Li-Ping
Wang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: In this report, we present the synthesis of yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres followed with the surface modification of carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (donated as Co3O4@CuO@GQDs) and investigate their lithium storage properties. Derived from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), the obtained yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres exhibit well-defined microstructure and high porosities. The yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO structure is designed to adapt the stepwise lithium insertion mechanism (first in CuO shell and then in Co3O4 core). In addition, the GQDs decorated on the surface of Co3O4@CuO microspheres not only provides larger specific surface area, more active sites and enhanced electronic conductivity, but also works as a buffer to alleviate the volume expansion and a reservoir for electrolyte molecules to improve the ionic conductivity. Furthermore, the –COOH groups from GQDs exhibits good hydrophilicity which is supposed to be useful for the combination of GQDs with Co3O4@CuO and also shows strong affinity to Li+. Based on the above merits from the structural and compositional design, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs anode displays enhanced cyclability and superior lithium storage performance. Specifically, compared to the bald Co3O4@CuO microspheres without GQDs which suffer from a severe capacity decline with an inferior capacity of 414 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs anode displays an initial specific capacity of 816 mAh g−1 and a high reversible charge capacity of 1054 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.1 A g−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cobalt oxide
Copper oxide
Yolk-shell
Lithium ion batteries

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) XRD patterns of Co3O4@CuO and Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. b) TG curve of Co-Cu-BTC. N2 absorption-desorption curve of c) Co3O4@CuO and d) Co3O4@CuO@GQDs.

a) SEM and b) TEM images of Co-Cu-BTC. c) SEM and d) TEM images of Co3O4@CuO. e) SEM and f) TEM images of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. g) and h) HRTEM images of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs (the GQDs are marked by yellow rings).
a) EDS spectrum of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and elemental mapping images of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs, b) SEM image, c) Cu, d) Co, e) O and f) C.
XPS spectra of the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs: a) survey spectrum. b) Co 2p spectrum. c) Cu 2p spectrum. d) C 1 s spectrum.

a) The first three cycles of CV curves and b) Charge and discharge potential profiles of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. c) Cyclic performance of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and pristine Co3O4@CuO at 0.1 A g−1. d) rate performance of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and pristine Co3O4@CuO.

a) CV curves of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs at various scan rates ranging from 0.2 to 1 mV s−1. b) Log (i) versus log(v) plots. c) Contribution of capacitive and diffusion controlled behaviors at various scan rates. d) CV curve with detailed pseudocapacitive contribution at 1 mV s−1. e) The calculated Li chemical diffusion coefficients based on the GITT. f) GITT curve of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and Co3O4@CuO.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots modification of yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres for boosted lithium storage performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, we present the synthesis of yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres followed with the surface modification of carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (donated as Co3O4@CuO@GQDs) and investigate their lithium storage properties. Derived from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), the obtained yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres exhibit well-defined microstructure and high porosities. The yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO structure is designed to adapt the stepwise lithium insertion mechanism (first in CuO shell and then in Co3O4 core). In addition, the GQDs decorated on the surface of Co3O4@CuO microspheres not only provides larger specific surface area, more active sites and enhanced electronic conductivity, but also works as a buffer to alleviate the volume expansion and a reservoir for electrolyte molecules to improve the ionic conductivity. Furthermore, the –COOH groups from GQDs exhibits good hydrophilicity which is supposed to be useful for the combination of GQDs with Co3O4@CuO and also shows strong affinity to Li+. Based on the above merits from the structural and compositional design, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs anode displays enhanced cyclability and superior lithium storage performance. Specifically, compared to the bald Co3O4@CuO microspheres without GQDs which suffer from a severe capacity decline with an inferior capacity of 414 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs anode displays an initial specific capacity of 816 mAh g−1 and a high reversible charge capacity of 1054 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.1 A g−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their low self-discharging property, long lifetime and high energy density, lithium ion batteries (LIBs) have been concerned as popular energy storage systems in portable devices, electric vehicles (EVs), and emergency power package [1–5]. Nevertheless, the most widely used LIBs anode material, i.e. the graphite, cannot satisfy the market requirement towards EVs or hybrid EVs with high energy densities, limited by its low theoretical capacity (372 mAh g−1). As a consequence, considerable efforts have been paid to searching the ideal electrode materials to improve the reversible capacity and cycling performance of LIBs [6]. Recently, transition metal oxides (TMO) or sulfides (TMS) such as CuOx [7,8], CoOx [3,9], MnOx [10–12], FeOx [13–15], NiOx [16,17], SnOx [18,19], AgInZnS [20], Bi2S3 [21] appear as potential competitors for the next generation of electrode materials in LIBs due to their large theoretical capacities (500–1000 mAh g−1) and abundant reserves with low cost. However, the practical use of TMOs is obstructed by their poor inherent conductivity, severe capacity fading and large volume change during cycling.

Take into account the above considerations, great efforts including the design of mixed metal oxides, special morphology and hierarchical porous nanostructures [22,23], have been devoted to addressing these problems. Mixed transition metal oxides, consisting of two or more metal oxides, are representatives of promising electrode materials in LIBs because they usually own a higher electronic conductivity and synergic effects of multiple metal species which may be hard to achieve for the single metal [24]. Moreover, novel nanostructured TMOs with various morphologies can also help to maintain the integrity of the electrode material and avert its disintegration during the charge-discharge process. Recently, some mixed metal oxides, especially Cu-based and Co-based oxides with special morphologies (e.g. nanofiber [25], rectangular pyramid [26], nanosheet [27], hollow microspheres [28,29], flower-like [30] and nanowire [31]) have been developed as anode materials and shown enhanced lithium storage property. All of these specific morphologies are supposed to relieve the mechanical stress that is associated with the large volume change during Li+ intercalation/deintercalation process. Particularly, the composites with yolk-shell structure attract extensive interest due to their advantages such as more active sites, more space for volume expansion/contraction and shorten Li+ diffusion distance.

In order to prepare composites with yolk-shell structure, utilizing metal organic frameworks (MOFs) as structure precursors is regarded as a realistic strategy. Briefly, the MOFs can be thought of as a coordination network in which metal ions/clusters and organic ligands are periodically bonded through coordination bonds. The unique composition, the hybrid structure at the atomic level, and the proper chemical stability of MOFs together provide advantages for the immobilization of TMOs nanostructures into a porous carbon framework with tunable topologies upon direct pyrolysis [32]. For instance, specific structured metal oxides/mixed metal oxides are possible to be prepared by pyrolyzing MOFs precursors in air. After annealing process, the obtained products can maintain similar structural merits from its MOF precursors [32–37].

As an emerging attractive carbon-based material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have exhibited their unique merits in many fields such as LED, supercapacitors [38–41], photocatalysis water-splitting [42,43] and solar cell [44–47]. Benefiting from high specific surface area, good conductivity, high mobility and good dispersibility in solvents, GQDs especially with certain functionalization are gradually applied to electrode materials for LIBs (e.g. VO2 [48], MoS2 [49], CuO [50], Sn [51], Si [52,53], NiO [54] et al{Ji, 2017 #687}) and play a role on the enhancement of conductivity, promotion for ion transport, and improvement in cycling performance.

Herein, we introduce the fabrication of GQDs coated Co3O4@CuO yolk-shell ternary materials which are derived from its bimetallic MOF precursor. The schematic preparation process of GQDs modified yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres (Co3O4@CuO@GQDs) is shown in Scheme 1 . Briefly, a Co(II)-1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate (referred as Co-BTC) is prepared by a microwave irradiation method. The obtained Co-BTC is then converted to Co-Cu-BTC by means of cation exchange process between Cu2+ and Co2+. Interestingly, subsequent annealing of these bimetallic MOFs in air can yield Co3O4@CuO microspheres with yolk-shell structure. After a further hydrothermal treatment, the combination of Co3O4@CuO and carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots is then realized to achieve the main product Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of bimetallic composition, unique yolk-shell structure and better conductivity due to GQDs coating, the electrode material displays an initial charge capacity of 816 mAh g−1 and maintains a high reversible capacity of 1054 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.1 A g−1.

Fig. S1 shows TEM image of GQDs-COOH, which exhibits a well-dispersed property and a diameter range from 10 to 100 nm. The insert of Fig. S1 depicts the GQDs solution under 365 nm UV light. The observed bright blue photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs is caused by the quantum size effect and the functional groups of GQDs-COOH. The FTIR spectra of GQDs-COOH and Co3O4@CuO@GQDs are shown in Fig. S2. The vibration peaks of C = O groups at ∼1500 cm−1 can be observed in both GQDs-COOH and Co3O4@CuO@GQDs sample, indicating the maintenance of functional groups in GQDs after solvent-thermal process during which GQDs-COOH was combined with Co3O4@CuO microsphere. In order to characterize the porous structure and specific surface area (SSA) of the as-prepared products, N2 absorption-desorption analysis was conducted. As Fig. 1c shows, the SSA of Co3O4@CuO was determined to be ∼12 m2 g−1 and the pore distribution (insert image of Fig. 1c) shows its mesoporous feature (2–50 nm). Note that the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs exhibits much higher SSA of 36 m2 g−1, almost three times of that for Co3O4@CuO with more obvious mesoporous feature (Fig. 1d). These porous properties inherited from the MOF precursors can facilitate the diffusion of electrolytes and shorten the transfer path of lithium ion. Moreover, the introduction of GQDs significantly increases the surface area of Co3O4@CuO and provides more active sites for lithium storage.

The morphologies of the Co-Cu-BTC, Co3O4@CuO and Co3O4@CuO@GQDs were observed by electron microscopes. As shown in Fig. 2 a, b, the Co-Cu-BTC presents a microspherical yolk-shell morphology with a diameter around 2–3 μm (with an inner core of ∼1 μm and an outer shell with a thickness of ∼0.1 μm) and a rough surface which expresses network-like porous nanostructure. Additionally, abundant nanosheets are observed vertically deposited onto the surface of microsphere, forming an urchin-like structure. After calcining the Co-Cu-BTC at 450 °C for 2 h in air, the diameter of Co3O4@CuO is reduced to ∼1 μm owning to the removal of organic ligands but the yolk-shell structure is well maintained (Fig. 2d). However, the urchin-like structure is almost disappeared (Fig. 2c) leaving a rough surface. After coating GQDs by hydrothermal method, the surface of microsphere gains more roughness, which can be ascribed to the presence of GQDs. Nevertheless, the structure of yolk-shell is still well preserved (Fig. 2e, f).

The HRTEM image of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs are shown in Fig. 2g and h. Lattice fingers are found at 0.272, 0.141, 0.187, 0.24 and 0.23 nm. The first three lattice fingers are corresponded to the (1 1 0), (
31
1 ¯
) and (
20
2 ¯
) lattice planes, respectively, and the latter two are assigned to the (3 1 1) and (2 2 2) planes of Co3O4. We also observed the lattice finger of GQDs (marked by yellow ellipse), indicating their combination with Co3O4@CuO. It is worth mentioning that there is almost no change in diameter and lattice finger of GQDs before and after the coating process, implying the structural integrity of GQDs during the solvothermal process. EDS and elemental mapping were applied to determine the component of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the microspheres are consisted of C, O, Co and Cu element and the atomic ratio of Co: Cu: C is about 0.74:0.2:1. The elemental mapping (Fig. 3b–f) clearly indicates the uniform distribution of C, O, Co and Cu element in Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. XPS measurement was further conducted to evaluate the chemical composition of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. A full spectrum of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs shown in Fig. 4 a exhibits the characteristic peaks corresponding to Co, Cu, C and O elements. As shown in Fig. 4b, the Co 2p spectrum exhibit two obvious peaks located at 780.2 eV and 795.4 eV, corresponding to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 and the energy separation of two peaks is about 15 eV. This result confirms the existence of Co2+ and Co3+ [3]. In addition, two distinct peaks located at 933.4 and 953.4 eV in Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 4c) can be respectively assigned to the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 spin-orbit states. And the energy separation is 20 eV, which is accordance with standard value of CuO [55]. Furthermore, based on the previous research [7,56], four types fitted peaks of C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d) located at 284.4, 285.0, 287.8 and 288.6 eV were observed and ascribed to CC, CO, CO and OCO bond, respectively, further proving the existence of GQDs-COOH. Based on the above results, the composite consists of Co3O4, CuO and GQDs-COOH and the atomic percent of Co: Cu: C was estimated to be about 0.48: 0.34: 1.

The detecting depth of XPS is just several nanometers while the detecting depth of EDS can be several microns. So we can combine the elemental analysis detected by EDS and XPS to determine the distribution of different elements. According to the atomic ratio that is estimated from EDS result (Co: Cu: C = 0.74:0.2:1) and XPS result (Co: Cu: C = 0.48:0.34:1), we can find the atomic ratio of Cu to C detected by XPS are apparently higher than that detected by EDS. Considering that the carbon element from GQDs is only presented on the surface of Co3O4@CuO microspheres and the XPS test gives more surface information than EDS, the above comparison of the atomic ratio from EDS and XPS data implies that the Cu element is more presented in the outer shell, but there is more Co element in the inner core of the microsphere, which is accordance with the surface ion exchange MOF-related preparation process.

In order to gain more details on the element distribution of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs, a broken Co3O4@CuO@GQDs microsphere was selected to conduct the line-scan EDS analysis along the radial direction. As shown in Fig. S5, more Co but less Cu elements are observed in the core section compared to those of the shell layer. These analysis results are in good agreement with the experimental material design of surfacial Cu ions exchange doping on the preformed Co-BTC spheres.

GCD and CV tests were conducted to investigate the lithium storage mechanism of the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and pristine Co3O4@CuO anode during cycling. Fig. 5 a shows the typical CV curve of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs for the first three cycles at 0.1 mV·s−1. During the first reduction scan, four cathodic peaks located at 2.02 V, 1.25 V, 1.03 V and 0.86 V are observed. In line with the previous literature [8,9,57,58], the reduction peaks located at 2.02 V and 1.25 V can be interpreted as the gradual reduction of CuO to Cu2O and the peak at 1.03 V indicates the reduction process of Co3O4 to Co. Then, at ∼0.86 V, the Cu2O is transformed into Cu accompanied by the generation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film and decomposing of the electrolytes. Note that this stepwise lithium insertion behavior, i.e. first in CuO shell and then in Co3O4 core during discharging process adapts well to the unique yolk-shell structure of Co3O4@CuO composite, which can benefit for the electrode performance. In the anodic sweep, a weak broad peak from 0.92 V to 1.72 V could be attributed to the oxidation of the metallic Cu and Co to Cu2O and CoO, respectively. The other two anodic peaks at 2.1 V and 2.43 V are ascribed to the complete oxidation of CoO and Cu2O to Co3O4 and CuO, respectively, along with the decomposition of Li2O. In the following two cycles, cathodic and oxidation peaks are shifted to slightly higher potentials and are nearly overlapped, revealing the highly reversibility of electrochemical reactions.

Fig. 5b shows the galvanostatic charging/discharging profiles of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs electrode for the first three cycles, the 100th and the 200th cycles at 0.1 A g−1 between 0.01 and 3.0 V. Obviously, there are three discharge potential plateaus (around 2.1, 1.4 and 1.0 V versus Li/Li+) in the 1st discharge curve, which can be corresponded to multistep reduction reactions of Co3O4@CuO to metallic Cu and Co and the formation of SEI film. In the first charging profile, the weak voltage plateaus at 1.2, 2.0 and 2.5 V versus Li/Li+ correspond to the gradual oxidation reaction of Co and Cu. It can be clearly observed that the initial discharge/charge capacities are 1352/816 mAh g−1, corresponding to 60% of the Coulomb efficiency (CE). For the following two cycles, a high CE approaching 100% is observed, showing the enhanced reversibility and outstanding cycle stability. Note that the discharge capacities and voltage plateaus varied after different cycles. The large decrease of the discharge capacity after the first cycle can be attributed to the decomposition of electrolytes and formation of SEI film in the first cycle. Then the discharge capacity was observed to increase after 100 and 200 cycles. This observation can be mainly due to the improved Li-diffusion kinetics by the gradual activation process with better electrolyte infiltration during cycling, although we admit the mechanism is not clear. In addition, the decrease of discharge platform after cycling can be ascribed to the decreased polarization of the electrode by improved kinetic during cycling.

The cycling performance of both the electrode Co3O4@CuO and Co3O4@CuO@GQDs at 0.1 A g−1 are also displayed. As showed in Fig. 5c, both materials show a rapid capacity increase in the first 15 cycles, indicating an activation process which has been also observed in previous reports [27,59]. Along with the increase of cycling time, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs electrode exhibits enhanced cycle stability. Almost no capacity fading is detected throughout the charging/discharging process and a reversible charging specific capacity of 1054 mAh g−1 is obtained after 200 cycles. By contrast, the Co3O4@CuO suffers a severe capacity fade from 15 cycles and obtains a relatively low capacity of 414 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles. Based on the observation mentioned above, the GQDs play a critical role on the promotion of the electrochemical performance of the electrode. It acts as conducting and buffering medium [49] for ionic/electronic transportation and significantly reduces the polarization degree and internal strain during cycling [50]. Fig. S3 displays the TG curve of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs, in which the obvious mass loss before 350 °C can be attributed to the oxidation process of GQDs. Therefore, the proportion of GQDs in the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs is estimated to be ∼11%.

To further understand the role of GQDs on the boosted lithium storage performance, electrochemical impedance tests were conducted for Co3O4@CuO and Co3O4@CuO@GQDs at frequencies of 100 kHz −0.01 Hz. The semicircle observed in the Nyquist plots of the spectrum is indicative of the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) between the electrode and the electrolyte. As depicted in Fig. S4, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs exhibits a much smaller diameter of semicircle than Co3O4@CuO, suggesting an enhanced electronic conductivity. Moreover, the slope of the line in the region of low frequency is related to the lithium-ion diffusion. Obviously, Co3O4@CuO@GQDs displayed a larger slope which suggests a larger Li+ diffusion rate compared to Co3O4@CuO electrode. This result indicates that the introduction of GQDs effectively promotes the ionic/electronic conductivity of Co3O4@CuO thus leading to a superior electrochemical performance.

Fig. 5d further shows the rate capacity of Co3O4@CuO and Co3O4@CuO@GQDs at gradually-increased charging/discharging rates from 0.1 to 5 A g−1. Obviously, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs anode displays a capacity of 921 mAh g−1 after 5 cycles at 0.1 A g−1. Upon increasing the current densities to 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A g−1, the corresponding charging capacities are observed to be 960, 940, 780, 610 and 380 mAh g−1, respectively. As the current rate is set back to 0.1 A g−1, a reversible capacity of 1050 mAh g−1 is achieved, which is still higher than those values observed during the initial 5 cycles. In comparison, the Co3O4@CuO electrode exhibits much lower average capacities of 889, 843, 741, 543, 368 and 202 mAh g−1 at the corresponding current rates, respectively. And the Co3O4@CuO electrode just recovers its capacity back to 633 mAh g−1, far below that of the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs electrode at same test conditions. The above phenomenon further confirms the advantage of GQDs on the enhancement of the stability of Co3O4@CuO electrode.

CV profiles at variable scan rates between 0.2 and 1 mV s−1 were conducted to obtain more insight on lithium storage mechanism of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the CV curves of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs display similar shapes indicating an excellent reversibility. As the sweep rate increases, the current intension of peaks elevates and the cathode/anode peak moves to the left/right, respectively. Moreover, the scan rate(
ν
) and current(i) follows the formula below [60]:
(1)
i = a
v b
where the value of coefficient b is related to the charge storage kinetics of the electrode. To be specific, when b approaches 0.5, it suggests that the storage kinetic is more controlled by ionic diffusion and the electrode shows more a battery behavior. While when b value goes to 1, it indicates that the storage kinetic is surface-controlled accompanying with a capacitive process [60,61]. As shown in Fig. 6b, the b-value of cathodic and anodic peaks are respectively calculated to be 0.88 and 0.77, indicating both a surface and diffusion-controlled process taken place in Co3O4@CuO@GQDs electrode during cycling.

In order to further quantify the contribution of capacitive behavior on the total charges stored at different sweep rates, the following equation could be used.
(2)
i
( V )
=
k
1
v +
k
2
v
1 / 2
where i(V) represent the measured current at fixed potential, k1 v and k2 v 1/2 can be interpreted as the contribution of capacitive and diffusion behavior, respectively [61]. The calculated percentages of contribution at various scan rates are shown in Fig. 6c. It demonstrates that the contribution of capacitive behavior is predominant and its contribution gradually increases as the scan rate increases. The percentage of capacitive contribution are 74%, 80%, 83%, 85% and 86% at scan rates of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1 mV s−1 respectively. The detailed pseudocapacitive contributions are displayed in the corresponding CV profile at 1 mV s−1 (Fig. 6d). The high capacitive contribution clearly points out that Co3O4@CuO@GQDs can provide affluent pathway for the transportation of electrons and therefore promote the lithium storage properties.

Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique (GITT) was also conducted to estimate the Li+ diffusion coefficient (DLi +) in Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and Co3O4@CuO anode during discharge process (Fig. 6f). Suppose that the Li+ diffusion process meets the Fick’s second law, then the DLi + can be evaluated by the following Eq. (3) [62]:
(3)
D
Li
=
4
π τ
m B
V m
A
M B
Δ
E s
Δ
E t
2
τ ≪
L
2
D
Li
where τ is defined as the titration time; the mass and molar mass of the active material are represented by mB and MB, respectively; Vm represents the molar volume; A refers to the surface area of anode, ΔEs and ΔEt represent the steady-state voltage change after a current disturbance and voltage change during the constant current titration; L corresponds to the length of diffusion pathway.

Based on the GITT profiles and Eq. (3), the calculated DLi + at different discharge state are shown in Fig. 6e. Obviously, Co3O4@CuO@GQDs exhibited a higher DLi + (1.36 × 10−12–8.86 × 10−15 cm2 s−1) than Co3O4@CuO (8.21 × 10−14–7.82 × 10−17 cm2 s−1). These values imply that the coating of GQDs can efficiently promote the diffusion of Li+. Indeed, the existence of GQDs on the surface of Co3O4@CuO is presumed to form a supporting layer that can store electrolytes and therefore reduce the transfer resistance of Li+ at the interface of electrolyte/electrode, resulting a shorten pathway for the diffusion of Li+. Furthermore, the abundant carboxyl groups of GQDs are supposed to provide more active sites to accelerate the diffusion rate of Li+ during cycling. SEM and TEM measurements were conducted to detect the morphology change of the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs electrode after 200 cycles as shown in Fig. S6. The yolk-shell internal void structure is hard to observe, which might be due to the existence of acetylene black and binding agent during the preparation of the electrode. Nevertheless, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs microspheres are still well maintained with their spherical morphology after 200 repetitive cycles, indicating the good structural stability of the electrode.

The observed good lithium storage performances can be explained by the following merits. First, the stepwise lithium storage mechanism, i.e. first in CuO shell then in Co3O4 core, adapts well to the unique yolk-shell structure of Co3O4@CuO microsphere and help to alleviate the structural expansion during cycling. Besides, the inherited mesoporous features arising from MOF precursors provide more surface areas therefore richer active sites for the lithium storage and meanwhile help for the diffusion of electrolytes and Li+ towards a good cyclability of the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs electrode. Moreover, the GQDs coating on the surface of the Co3O4@CuO microsphere plays also significant roles on the electrochemical performances. On the one hand, as a good conductor and a reservoir for electrolytes, it efficiently enhances the ionic/electronic conductivity of the electrode composite therefore promotes the reaction kinetics of the electrode. One the other hand, it provides more surface areas and active sites for lithium storage. The hydrophilic carboxyl functional groups from GQDs show also good affinity to polar ions and therefore further promote the diffusion of Li+ during cycling. Consequently, the inherent properties of the Co3O4@CuO yolk-shell microspheres together with the effective assistance of GQDs coatings boost their electrochemical performances of the anode in LIBs.

In summary, GQDs coated Co3O4@CuO yolk-shell microspheres (Co3O4@CuO@GQDs) are prepared via microwave-assisted solvothermal method towards an enhanced lithium storage behavior. Co-Cu-BTC MOF precursors were synthesized and used as structural and composition precursors to generate the yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres. GQDs were then subsequently introduced onto the surface of the microspheres. The obtained Co3O4@CuO@GQDs electrode exhibits remarkably improved lithium storage performance compared to the Co3O4@CuO electrode without modification of GQDs. Specifically, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs displays an initial specific capacity of 816 mAh g−1 and a high reversible charge capacity of 1054 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.1 A g−1. In comparison, the bald Co3O4@CuO microspheres suffer from severe capacity decay since 15 cycles and keep only a small capacity of 414 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles. The boosted lithium storage behavior of Co3O4@CuO@GQDs can be first attributed to its high porosity, unique yolk-shell structure, and the multistep Li+ storage process that alleviates the structural expansion of the electrode during cycling. In addition, the introduction of GQDs further provides more specific surface areas and enhances the electronic conductivity of Co3O4@CuO, thus leading to more exposed active sites as well as a faster transportation process of electrons in the electrode. Moreover, the functional carboxyl groups from GQDs that immobilize firmly on the surface of Co3O4@CuO are also supposed to have strong affinity to Li+ and shorten the ion transport pathway.

Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, K30), cobaltous nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2 6H2O) and copper (ii) nitrate hydrate (Cu(NO3)·3H2O) with the purity degree of 98% are purchased from Sinopharm. Graphene quantum dots with carboxy groups (GQDs, 80%) is obtained from XF Nano. 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC, 98%) and N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99.5%) are provided by Alfa Aesar and Chemical Reagent, respectively. 1 M of LiPF6 dissolved in the mixed solvent ethylene carbonate(EC)/diethyl carbonate (DEC) (1:1, w/w) is prepared by Guotai Huarong Chemical New Material Co., Ltd. All the above chemicals were used as received if not further stated.

Typically, H3BTC (0.131 g) and PVP (0.265 g) were dissolved in 10 mL methanol with stirring at room temperature (R.T.) to obtain a clear solution labelled as solution A. Co(NO3)·6H2O (10 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL methanol and stirred at R.T. labelled as solution B. After stirring for 30 min, the solution B was dropped slowly into the solution A and further stirred for 30 min. Afterwards, the mixed solution was put in a microwave reactor (Nova, EU Microwave Chemistry) and reacted at 150 °C for 30 min with constant stirring to obtain the Co-BTC precursor. In the next moment, a 5 mmol of Cu(NO3)·3H2O was first mixed with the as-synthesized Co-BTC dispersion under stirring for 30 min and then reacted in the same microwave reactor at 150 °C for another 30 min. After cooling to R.T., the obtained product was purified with methanol and ethanol by centrifuge and then heated at 60 °C to obtain dry Co-Cu-BTC.

Yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microsphere was obtained by annealing Co-Cu-BTC in air. Typically, the Co-Cu-BTC precursor was ground into powder and then dispersed in a porcelain boat. Afterwards, it was annealed at 450 °C for 2 h in air. After naturally cooling to the ambient temperature, the yielded black powder (the yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres) were obtained.

To synthesize Co3O4@CuO@GQDs composite, GQDs-COOH and Co3O4@CuO were added in 10 mL of DI water with a mass ratio of 1: 5 and sonicated for 30 min. Then the mixture dispersion was placed into 25 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and held at 150 °C for 4 h. After cooling, it was centrifuged and purified at R.T. for several times and then dried under vacuum at 80 °C to get the final product Co3O4@CuO@GQDs.

The phase, morphological and structure characterization of yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and Co3O4@CuO were conducted by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PUXI XD-3), scanning/transmission electron microscopy (SEM/TEM, JSM-7500F, JEM-2010F), respectively. A ThermoFischer (ESCALAB 250Xi) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Micromeritics ASAP 2020 M + C analyzer was used to evaluate the surface compositions and specific surface area and porous structure of the yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO@GQDs materials. A NETZSCHSTA 409 PG/PC thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) was used to measure the thermostability and decomposition behavior of the product.

The electrochemical performances of yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO@GQDs and yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO were evaluated in CR2032 half-cells fabricated in a glove box filled with Ar. The content of H2O/O2 in the glove box is kept below 1 ppm. The working electrode consists of 60% active material (yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO@GQDs or yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO), 20% conductive agent (acetylene black) and 20% binder (PVDF). The weight of anode material loading on the copper foil is about 1.5 mg·cm−2. A foil of lithium was used as a reference electrode while 1 M LiPF6 solution was deployed as electrolyte. The galvanostatic charging-discharging behavior in a voltage range of 0.005–3 V at 0.1A·g−1 was examined using a LAND-CT2001 test system. A CHI660D electrochemical workstation was used to acquire the cyclic voltammetry (0.1 mV·s−1) and Nyquist plots (100 kHz-0.01 Hz).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51603119), Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission (17010500300, 16YF1404200), Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Innovation Program (2019-01-07-00-09-E00021), QD2016027, 16CG46) and Innovative Research Team of High-level Local Universities in Shanghai for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.05.098.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
